verses.commentary: A man named Lazarus was sick. He was from Bethany, the village of Mary and her sister Martha. Location and family identified. Bethany near Jerusalem. The sisters introduced.

This Mary, whose brother Lazarus now lay sick, was the same one who poured perfume on the Lord and wiped His feet with her hair. Forward reference to John 12. John assumes readers know the story. Mary characterized by worship.
So the sisters sent word to Jesus, Lord, the one You love is sick. Simple message. No explicit request. They assume His love means action. Relationship implies response.

When He heard this, Jesus said, This sickness will not end in death. No, it is for God's glory so that God's Son may be glorified through it. Prophetic statement. Death is not the end. Purpose: divine glory. The Son will be glorified. Death serves revelation.
Deep affection. What follows is not indifference but intentional delay.
Love delays. Strategic waiting. Greater glory through death and resurrection than immediate healing.
And then He said to His disciples, Let us go back to Judea. Decision to return. Dangerous territory. Recent stoning attempt (10:31). Courage required.
But Rabbi, a short while ago the Jews there tried to stone You, and yet You are going back? Disciples protest. Reasonable fear. Why return to danger? Self-preservation versus mission.

Are there not twelve hours of daylight? Anyone who walks in the daytime will not stumble, for they see by this world's light. Metaphor. Daylight represents God's appointed time. Walking in His will brings safety. Divine timing protects.
It is when a person walks at night that they stumble, for they have no light. Night walking is dangerous. Outside God's will brings peril. Timing is crucial.

After He had said this, He went on to tell them, Our friend Lazarus has fallen asleep; but I am going there to wake him up. Sleep as euphemism for death. Jesus will awaken him. Resurrection foreshadowed in language.
His disciples replied, Lord, if he sleeps, he will get better. Literal misunderstanding. Sleep aids recovery. They miss the metaphor. Natural versus spiritual thinking.
Death, not rest. Communication gap.
So then He told them plainly, Lazarus is dead. Direct statement. No metaphor. Plain speech.
And for your sake I am glad I was not there, so that you may believe. But let us go to him. Glad for their sake. Delay served their faith. Greater miracle builds stronger belief. Pedagogical purpose.

Then Thomas (also known as Didymus) said to the rest of the disciples, Let us also go, that we may die with Him. Loyalty mixed with pessimism. Thomas commits to Jesus even unto death. Courageous but dark expectation.
Beyond resuscitation. Jewish belief: soul lingers three days, then departs. Lazarus is definitively dead. Decay begun.
Many Jerusalem Jews would come. Public miracle. Wide witness.
Shiva. Crowd present. Witnesses to grief and soon to glory.
Martha active, Mary contemplative. Both grieve differently.
Lord, if You had been here, my brother would not have died. Gentle accusation. Faith in His power. Regret at His absence. She believes He could have prevented death.
But I know that even now God will give You whatever You ask. Remarkable faith. Even now: not too late. God will grant Jesus' requests. Belief in ongoing power.
Jesus said to her, Your brother will rise again. Promise of resurrection. Ambiguous timing. Future certain.
Martha answered, I know he will rise again in the resurrection at the last day. Orthodox belief. Final resurrection. Daniel 12:2. But she thinks distant future, not immediate.

Jesus said to her, I am the resurrection and the life. The one who believes in Me will live, even though they die. Fifth 'I AM' statement. Jesus is not just agent of resurrection but resurrection itself. Life inherent in Him. Believers transcend physical death.
And whoever lives by believing in Me will never die. Do you believe this? Spiritual death defeated. Eternal life now. Question demands response. Personal faith required.

Yes, Lord, I believe that You are the Messiah, the Son of God, who is to come into the world. Creedal confession. Threefold title: Messiah, Son of God, Coming One. Martha's confession rivals Peter's (Matthew 16:16).
After she had said this, she went back and called her sister Mary aside. The Teacher is here, and is asking for you. She summons Mary. Teacher: personal title. Jesus requests her presence.
Devotion drives her. She goes to Jesus.
Jesus waits outside. Privacy for encounter.
Assumption: mourning ritual. Actually: meeting Messiah. Public witness to private encounter.

When Mary reached the place where Jesus was and saw Him, she fell at His feet and said, Lord, if You had been here, my brother would not have died. Worship posture. Same words as Martha. Shared grief, shared faith. Both sisters believe His presence prevents death.
Anger at death's ravages? Troubled: tarasso (ταράσσω), agitated. Jesus emotionally affected. Full humanity.
Where have you laid him? Come and see, Lord. Jesus asks location. They invite Him to the tomb. Parallel to 1:39, Come and see.
(ἐδάκρυσεν), shed tears. Shortest verse in Scripture. Profound theology. God weeps. Incarnation includes tears. Compassion, grief, full humanity displayed.
Then the Jews said, See how He loved him! They interpret tears as love. Correct reading. Affection genuine. Love made visible.

But some of them said, Could not He who opened the eyes of the blind man have kept this man from dying? Chapter 9 remembered. Logic: healing power should prevent death. They miss the greater plan. Resurrection exceeds prevention.
Tomb described. Cave sealed with stone. Burial place.
Take away the stone. But, Lord, by this time there is a bad odor, for he has been there four days. Command given. Martha objects. Decay expected. Stench of death. Four days confirms decomposition.
Then Jesus said, Did I not tell you that if you believe, you will see the glory of God? Rebuke. Promise recalled (verse 4). Faith precedes sight. Glory coming. Belief required.

So they took away the stone. Then Jesus looked up and said, Father, I thank You that You have heard Me. Stone removed. Prayer begins. Thanksgiving precedes request. Already heard. Past tense. Prayer answered before spoken.

I knew that You always hear Me, but I said this for the benefit of the people standing here, that they may believe that You sent Me. Constant communion. Father always hears. Public prayer for witness. Proof of divine mission. Sent by the Father.

When He had said this, Jesus called in a loud voice, Lazarus, come out! Command to the dead. Loud voice: authority. Specific name: if unnamed, all dead would rise. Power over death. Creation word speaks again.

The dead man came out, his hands and feet wrapped with strips of linen, and a cloth around his face. Jesus said to them, Take off the grave clothes and let him go. Lazarus emerges. Still bound. Alive but restricted. Jesus commands liberation. Community helps unwrap. Resurrection requires witnesses and helpers.
Eyewitnesses believe. Miracle validates. Many converted.
Miracle produces two responses: faith and fear. Some report to authorities. Hostility persists despite evidence.

Then the chief priests and the Pharisees called a meeting of the Sanhedrin. What are we accomplishing? Here is this man performing many signs. Emergency council. Admission of powerlessness. Signs accumulate. They cannot stop Him.

If we let Him go on like this, everyone will believe in Him, and then the Romans will come and take away both our temple and our nation. Political calculation. Fear of popular movement. Roman intervention threatened. Messiah brings trouble. Self-preservation over truth.

Then one of them, named Caiaphas, who was high priest that year, spoke up, You know nothing at all! Arrogance. Dismissal of colleagues. High priest that year: John notes the rotation. Caiaphas in power AD 18-36.

You do not realize that it is better for you that one man die for the people than that the whole nation perish. Pragmatic solution. Sacrifice one to save many. Utilitarian calculus. Political expediency. Unintentional prophecy.
Prophetic office speaks truth through wicked man. God uses even opponents. Jesus will indeed die for the nation.
Not just Jews. Scattered children: Gentiles to be gathered. Universal atonement. One people from many nations. Ephesians 2 foreshadowed.
Conspiracy formalized. Death sentence pronounced. Lazarus' resurrection seals Jesus' fate. Irony: giver of life condemned to death.
No longer public. Near wilderness. Final retreat before Passion. Time with disciples.
Pilgrims gather. Purification rites. The stage is set for final confrontation.

They kept looking for Jesus, and as they stood in the temple courts they asked one another, What do you think? Isn't He coming to the festival at all? Expectation. Searching. Will He risk appearing? Public curiosity.
Informants recruited. Manhunt. Authorities actively seeking to capture. The hour draws near.
